title_en: Al-Ghazali on the Sources of Islamic Law
content_en:
At the heart of the Mustasfa is al-Ghazali's treatment of the four sources of Islamic law — Quran, Sunnah, ijma (scholarly consensus), and qiyas (analogical reasoning) — and their arrangement in a strict hierarchy of authority. This hierarchy is not arbitrary: it reflects al-Ghazali's epistemological conviction that legal certainty must track the certainty of the underlying evidence, and that different sources of law command different degrees of epistemic confidence.

The Quran stands first and is the primary ground of all Islamic legal rulings. For al-Ghazali, the Quran's authority is absolute because its transmission is mutawatir — conveyed by so many chains of narrators across every generation that the possibility of collective fabrication or error is rationally excluded. Any ruling established by a clear, unambiguous Quranic text (nass) is legally certain: it admits no qualification by weaker evidence and is not subject to abrogation by anything short of another Quranic text or a similarly certain hadith.

The Sunnah occupies the second rank. Al-Ghazali's treatment here is nuanced and reflects his training in both hadith criticism and Shafi'i legal methodology. He distinguishes carefully between mutawatir hadiths — those transmitted by so many parallel chains that they produce certain knowledge — and ahad reports, which are conveyed through fewer chains and produce only probable knowledge (zann). Mutawatir hadiths carry authority comparable to the Quran in legal matters; ahad reports, while sufficient to establish legal rulings (including obligation) in the Shafi'i view, do not produce the same certainty. Al-Ghazali devotes considerable attention to how the Sunnah functions as an explanatory and supplementary source alongside the Quran — clarifying its general principles, specifying its ambiguous terms, and adding rulings for matters the Quran addresses only implicitly.

Ijma, the consensus of qualified Muslim scholars, occupies the third rank. Al-Ghazali regards properly established ijma as legally binding and not subject to revision by later ijtihad. His argument for ijma's authority rests on a hadith — the Prophet's statement that his community would not agree upon an error — combined with a theoretical case that the collective judgment of the scholars functions as a safeguard against deviation. He is careful, however, to restrict the binding force of ijma to cases where consensus is genuine and verifiable: claimed consensus that is actually disputed, or that rests on the silence of scholars who may simply have been unaware of the question, does not qualify.

Qiyas, analogical reasoning from established cases to new ones, holds the fourth rank. Al-Ghazali's treatment of qiyas is the most technically detailed portion of this section of the Mustasfa: he analyses the four elements of a valid analogy (the established case, the new case, the ruling of the established case, and the ratio legis that the two cases share), the methods for identifying and verifying the operative ratio, and the conditions under which an analogy can be overridden by a more specific text or a contrary analogy.

Crucially, al-Ghazali explicitly and firmly rejects istihsan — juristic preference — as an independent source of law. This is his sharpest point of disagreement with Hanafi methodology. For al-Ghazali, allowing a jurist to depart from a sound analogy on the grounds that the analogical result seems harsh or counterintuitive opens the door to arbitrary reasoning dressed up as legal method. Every departure from a sound qiyas requires a specific textual or rational justification — preference alone is insufficient. This rejection of istihsan is one of the defining methodological positions of the Shafi'i school, and al-Ghazali defends it with characteristic rigor in the Mustasfa.
